coredata.dc:title: Comparison of N-doped carbon dots synthesized from the main components of plants including cellulose, lignin, and xylose: Characterized, fluorescence mechanism, and potential applications
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Xiufen
Chen, Congjin
Zhou, Ruxia
Huang, Quanlong
Liang, Qinglong
Huang, Zuqiang
Zhang, Yanjuan
Hu, Huayu
Liang, Yanglin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized from the main components of plants, including cellulose (N-cCDs), lignin (N-lCDs), and xylose (N-xCDs), were compared. TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS were carried out to characterize their morphology and chemical composition. Results show that the three obtained N-CDs contained N-doped sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon core, which was surrounded by oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. The three N-CDs exhibited a novel double excitation-one emission phenomenon, and their fluorescence were significantly enhanced by N-doping. Moreover, this study indicated that high lignin-containing biomass is not appropriate for producing CDs by hydrothermal method. Cellulose is conducive to produce CDs, while it needs a relatively high temperature and a long time. Low-molecular carbohydrate-rich biomass and nitrogen-containing biomass is the better carbon source to produce the CDs. Also, this study revealed that N-cCDs and N-xCDs are excellent candidates for fluorescent ink, fabrics identifying, temperature sensing, and Hg2+ sensing. Due to its poor fluorescence property, N-lCDs is not suitable for fluorescent ink and fabrics identification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Main components of plants
Fluorescence mechanism
Application

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of pH and high salt concentration on N-CDs
Potential applications of N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and HRTEM images (inset) of N-cCDs (a), N-lCDs (b) and N-xCDs (c). SEM images of cellulose (d), lignin (e) and xylose (f), SEM images of reactive the residues of raw cellulose (g), lignin (h) and xylose (i).
XRD patterns of the three N-CDs.
Comparison of FT-IR spectra of the three N-CDs.
XPS surveys of N-cCDs (a), N-lCDs (b) and N-xCDs (c). C1s, N1s, O1s high resolution XPS surveys of N-cCDs (a-1~ a-3), N-lCDs (b-1~ b-3), and N-xCDs (c-1 ~ c-3).

Excitation and emission spectra of cCDs and N-cCDs (a), lCDs and N-lCDs (b), and xCDs and N-xCDs (c). Both of the excitation and emission slit of cCDs, N-cCDs, xCDs and N-xCDs were 5 nm, and that of lCDs and N-lCDs were 10 nm. The concentration of cCDs, N-cCDs, xCDs and N-xCDs was 2.0 μg/mL, and that of lCDs and N-lCDs was 4.0 μg/mL.
The fluorescence mechanism of CDs and N-CDs.

Comparison of fluorescence intensity of the three CDs before and after the introduction of NH4HCO3 (a). Comparison the fluorescence of the three N-CDs synthesized from different nitrogen sources (b). All the fluorescence measurements were carried out at the excitation wavelength of 230 nm. The concentration of CDs and N-CDs was 2.0 μg/mL. Both of the excitation and emission slit widths were 5 nm.

Influence of pH (a) and NaCl concentration (b) to the three N-CDs. All the FL measurements were carried out at the excitation wavelength of 230 nm. Both of the excitation and emission slit widths were 5 nm. The concentration of N-cCDs and N-xCDs was 2.0 μg/mL, that of N-lCDs was 4.0 μg/mL.

The linear relationship of N-cCDs and N-xCDs FL quenching intensity versus temperature ranging from 9 °C to 80 °C. Inset: the FL intensity of N-lCDs is plotted versus temperature. The excitation and emission slit width were both of 5 nm for N-cCDs and N-xCDs, and of 10 nm for N-lCDs. The concentration of all the N-CDs was 2.0 μg/mL.

a: Fluorescence graph of three N-CDs under 365 nm UV light. b: Fluorescence text written in a paper under 365 nm UV light using the three N-CDs. c: Photograph of origin cotton, silk, and eiderdown fabric under 365 nm and 254 nm UV light, and the fabrics treated with the three N-CDs under 254 nm UV light.

Fluorescence spectra of the three N-CDs (a ~ c) exposed to different amounts of Hg2+ (0, 25, 75, 125, 175, 275, 375, 500, 625, 750, 1000 nM for N-cCDs; 0, 25, 50, 100, 150, 250, 350, 500, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500 nM for N-lCDs, 0, 25, 50, 100, 150, 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1250 nM for N-xCDs). The linear plot of FL intensity ratios of F0/F versus the Hg2+ concentrations (d). Selectivity studies of the three N-CDs toward different metal ions (1 μM) (e). All the fluorescence measurements were carried out at the excitation wavelength of 230 nm with N-CDs concentration of 2.0 μg/mL. The excitation and emission slit width were both of 5 nm for N-cCDs and N-xCDs, and of 10 nm for N-lCDs.
Comparison of the optimal hydrothermal conditions and the corresponding fluorescence quantum yields of N-CDs.
Comparison of the Stokes shift of different CDs and this work.

row.entry: CDs from 3-aminophenyboronic acid and citric acid
CDs from N-aminoethylpiperazine and citric acid
CDs from trisodium citrate and l-Cysteine

head.ce:title: Comparison of N-doped carbon dots synthesized from the main components of plants including cellulose, lignin, and xylose: Characterized, fluorescence mechanism, and potential applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Design and Research Institute of Guangxi University, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Design and Research Institute of Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Design and Research Institute of Guangxi university, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Center of Nanning Customs District, Nanning, 530021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technical Center of Nanning Customs District

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical Center of Nanning Customs District, Nanning, 530021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized from the main components of plants, including cellulose (N-cCDs), lignin (N-lCDs), and xylose (N-xCDs), were compared. TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS were carried out to characterize their morphology and chemical composition. Results show that the three obtained N-CDs contained N-doped sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon core, which was surrounded by oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. The three N-CDs exhibited a novel double excitation-one emission phenomenon, and their fluorescence were significantly enhanced by N-doping. Moreover, this study indicated that high lignin-containing biomass is not appropriate for producing CDs by hydrothermal method. Cellulose is conducive to produce CDs, while it needs a relatively high temperature and a long time. Low-molecular carbohydrate-rich biomass and nitrogen-containing biomass is the better carbon source to produce the CDs. Also, this study revealed that N-cCDs and N-xCDs are excellent candidates for fluorescent ink, fabrics identifying, temperature sensing, and Hg2+ sensing. Due to its poor fluorescence property, N-lCDs is not suitable for fluorescent ink and fabrics identification.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the youngest members in the carbon material family [1]. Since it was initially found in 2004 [2], the CDs have been rapidly developed in this decade. When compared with similar materials, CDs possesses comprehensive advantages [3] including high stability, outstanding water-solubility, optical properties and optoelectrical conversion ability. Although semiconductor-based quantum dots (CdTe, PbTe et al.) show rival abilities of water-solubility, fluorescence, and optoelectrical conversion, they often contain toxic and harmful elements (Cd, Pb, and S et al.), which are not friendly to the environment. Although noble metal nanoparticles such as Ag NPs and Au NPs also show the good biocompatibility, they often suffer from the drawback of low fluorescence quantum yield (QY). Now, CDs have become a remarkable material due to its distinctive properties and potential applications in fluorescence devices [4,5], optical/electrical sensors [6,7], cell imaging [8,9], catalysis [10,11] et al.

Biomass, which is eco-friendly, renewable, inexpensive, and contains a high content of carbon and abundance of functional group, has been an outstanding carbon source for synthesizing CDs [11–13]. To date, various kinds of biomass-based materials, such as fruit juice [10], sawdust [14], plant leaves [15,16] and so on, have been used to produce CDs. However, the properties, components, and structure of CDs produced from different carbon precursors are quite various. Moreover, the QY of most biomass-derived CDs was low, which might restrict their application [8]. Therefore, the selection of potential precursor remains a crucial challenge for the scalable production of CDs. Study on the CDs synthesized from the main components of the plants (such as cellulose, lignin and xylose) is of great significance in understanding the properties of CDs and for the selection of a suitable carbon source from plant biomass. Although CDs synthesized from cellulose, xylose, and lignin have been reported [17–20], their properties were difficult to be compared because of the different synthetic methods.

Though CDs are a hot-studied material, its fluorescence mechanism remains controversial [21,22]. Xiong et al. [2] reviewed the influence of molecular fluorophores on CDs and emphasized that organic fluorophores in CDs play a crucial role in its optical properties. Zhu et al. [22] summarized four reasonable fluorescence mechanisms for CDs, which are quantum confinement effect, surface state, molecule state, and crosslink-enhanced emission. Ray and colleges [23] found the sized-dependent properties of CDs, the smaller nanoparticle is, the higher QY. Besides, Li et al. revealed that the isolated sp2 conjugated carbon clusters promote the fluorescence of CDs [24]. The theories mentioned above have their own advantages and scopes but fail to fit in all circumstances as the variety of CDs. Therefore, there is a great need to further explore the fluorescence mechanism of CDs for the development of fast and straightforward synthesis routes and apply them in the novel fields.

In this paper, cellulose, lignin, and xylose were selected as the carbon source for simultaneously producing CDs, and the products were denoted as cCDs, lCDs and xCDs, respectively. Their fluorescence (FL) intensities were enhanced by N-doping, and the corresponding N-doped CDs were marked as N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs. The TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, fluorescence spectra were carried out to comparatively characterize the morphology, structures, chemical composition, and optical properties of the three N-CDs. The novel dual excitation-one emission phenomenon and the dramatic enhancement of fluorescence induced by N-doping inspired us to investigate their fluorescence mechanism. The pronounced fluorescence properties of N-cCDs and N-xCDs promised them potential application in fluorescence link and identified the eiderdown fabric. Their fluorescence quenching intensity (ΔF) are linear responded to the temperature promising them as candidates for temperature sensing. Fluorescence quenching ratios (F
0
/F) of all three N-CDs are linear responded to Hg2+, so this toxic ions can be quantified in three new ways.

Microcrystalline cellulose powder, xylose, and lignin alkali powder were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Technology Co. Ltd, China. Hg2+ standard solution (100 μg/mL) was purchased from the National Center of Analysis and Testing for Nonferrous Metals and Electronic Materials (Beijing, China), and diluted into 1 μg/mL (5 μM) for further used. Ammonium carbonate (NH4HCO3), urea (CH4N2O), dicyandiamide (C2H4N4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) and other metal salts were purchased from the local regent company in China. All of these reagents were of analytical grade or higher. Ultrapure water prepared by the Milli-Q water purification system was used throughout the experiment.

The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured using a LS55 spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence measurements were carried out in pH 6.8 ultrawater at room temperature (Except temperature sensing). The morphology of CDs was characterized through a JEM-F200 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). The morphology of the reaction residues and the raw carbon source was performed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded via an X'Pert Pro MPD diffraction meter in the range of 10°–80°. The functional group of CDs was analyzed by a Nicolet iS10 Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were applied to characterize the elemental composition of CDs by a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi-technique surface analysis instrument. The FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, U.K.) was carried out to record decay curves of the three N-CDs with the absence and presence of Hg2+.

A certain amounts of carbon sources (cellulose, xylose, and lignin) and N sources (NH4HCO3, NH4OH, CON2H4, and C2H4N4) were dropped into a series of 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclaves sequentially. Then 20 mL ultrapure water was added, followed by treating with ultrasound wave at 700 W for 10 min. The reaction was carried out at 140, 160, 180, 200, or 210 oC for 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 12, 14, or 16 h, respectively. After the reaction, the autoclaves were cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained N-CDs were filtrated through 0.22 μm filter and dialyzed for 24 h with dialysis tube (MWCO: 1000 Da).

For fabric identification, a specific amount of eiderdown, cotton, and silk fabrics were directly dipped in the N-CDs solution, and dried naturally. The dipped-fabrics and the original fabrics were then observed at 365 nm and 254 nm UV light.

The sensing of temperature was performed as follows: 50 μL 0.4 mg/mL of N-CDs were put into difference tubes, and diluted to 10 mL with pH 6.8 ultrapure water. After that, the tubes were incubated at 9, 15, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 oC for 10 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded via LS55 fluorescence spectroscopy at the excitation wavelength of 230 nm.

Typically, 50 μL 0.4 mg/mL N-CDs solution and 5 mL of ultrapure water with pH 6.8 were mixed in the tubes. Series of Hg2+ ions (0, 50, 150, 250, 350, 550, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500, 2000 μL for N-cCDs; 0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 500, 700, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000 μL for N-lCDs; 0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500 μL for N-xCDs) with concentration of 5 μM were successively added. Then the mixtures were diluted to 10 mL with pH 6.8 ultrapure water. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 230 nm.

Hydrothermal conditions, including reaction temperature, reaction times, pH, reaction still volume, and the amount of carbon source were optimized by evaluating the fluorescence intensity (FL intensity). The optimal conditions and the corresponding QY for N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs were illustrated in Table 1 . Harnessing quinine sulfate (Φs = 54%) as the reference [3,16,18,25], the QYs of N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs were calculated to be 32.3%, 2.8%, and 35.0%, respectively, which were much higher than those of N-undoped cCDs (7.8%), lCDs (1.0%), and xCDs (8.0%). The corresponding fluorescence spectra of quinine sulfate, N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs in QY measurement are illustrated in Fig. S1.

From the above results, N-doping can efficiently improve the fluorescence of CDs. Cellulose and xylose are both good carbon sources for synthesis of CDs, but cellulose require higher temperature and longer reaction time to destroy its crystalline structure. CDs are difficult to be produced by lignin via hydrothermal process. Though the fluorescence of lCDs was increased 2.8 times by N-doping, it was still too weak for application.

The morphology and nanoparticle sizes of the three N-CDs were characterized by HRTEM images. Fig. 1 shows the good dispersibility of the three N-CDs (Fig. 1 a~c). All the three N-CDs exhibit nearly sphere-like shape and the average particle sizes were measured to be 5.45 nm, 6.63 nm, and 2.72 nm, respectively. Both (002) and (100) graphitic in-plane lattice with an interlayer spacing of 0.33 nm and 0.23 nm [26,27] were observed in N-cCDs and N-lCDs, and (100) graphitic in-plane lattice with interlayer spacing 0.23 nm was observed in N-xCDs (Inset of Fig. 1 a-c). These results suggest that graphene-like carbon cores were formed after hydrothermal treatment.

In order to investigate the formation of the three N-CDs intuitively, the SEM characterization was carried out for their corresponding reaction residues and carbon sources. Different shapes of microcrystalline cellulose can be found in both of the reaction residues and carbon sources of N-cCDs (Fig. 1d and g). Some agglomerated CDs can also be observed in its reaction residue (Fig. 1 d). The results show that cellulose can be decomposed into CDs by hydrothermal treatment. SEM image of lignin (Fig. 1h) shows large numbers of small flakes. After hydrothermal treatment, large compact flakes and some holes are observed in the reaction residue of lignin (Fig. 1e), which are supposed to be the imprints left by the stripping of N-lCDs. Results indicate that lignin will combine into thick flakes during hydrothermal treatment, resulting in N-lCDs difficult to be peeled off. This is the reason for the low QY of N-lCDs. The morphology of xylose exhibits massive particles (Fig. 1i), which can be dissolved into tiny molecules in water. After the hydrothermal treatment, these small molecules will aggregate into CDs, leaving behind some small particles (Fig. 1f).

The XRD patterns of the three N-CDs in Fig. 2 show distinct diffraction peaks of 28.9° for N-cCDs, 24.9° for N-lCDs, and 24.2° for N-xCDs. They are in agreement with the 002 plane of graphite. Additionally, the weak peaks of the three N-CDs (44.1°for N-cCDs, 43.9° for N-lCDs, and 43.1° for N-xCDs) corresponding to the typical graphitic interlayer stacking (100) peak. The XRD patterns further demonstrate the graphene-like carbon cores of three N-CDs. The broad XRD peaks indicate highly disordered carbon atoms in N-CDs [6,10,28]. The difference in the XRD pattern among the three N-CDs is probably caused by the fact that their carbon cores are not pure graphite carbon, instead, they may contain different sp3 hybrid carbon, and nitrogen-, oxygen-containing functional groups.

FT-IR analysis was performed to compare the functional groups among the three N-CDs, reaction residues, and carbon sources. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the typical bands for aromatic carbon clusters with sp2 structure are observed, including the stretching vibration for CC/CN at 1640 cm−1 and the out-plane bending vibration of aromatic C–H at around 860 cm−1 [29]. The peaks at approximately 2850 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of C–H from sp3 carbon. Besides, the characteristic stretching vibration of O–H at about 3420 cm−1, the stretching vibration bonds of C–O at 1230–935 cm−1, and the out-plane bending vibration of O–H at approximately 700 cm−1 demonstrate that the three N-CDs exist oxygen-containing functional groups. Furthermore, peaks at approximately 3200 cm−1 and 700 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration and the out-plane bending vibration of N–H. Strong peaks located at 1405 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1 might be the bending vibration of quaternary ammonium and amine group, and 1640 cm−1 could be assigned to the CN stretching vibration, implying the presence of amino nitrogen and aromatic nitrogen structures [30].

The comparison of FT-IR spectra among N-CDs, reaction residues, and carbon sources are shown in Fig. S2. FT-IR spectra of the three N-CDs exhibit noticeable change compared with their carbon source, indicating the dramatic transformation of their structure. The characteristic of FT-IR spectra of cellulose and lignin residues is similar to those of their carbon sources, while the residues of xylose shows identical characteristics to N-xCDs. These FT-IR results are consistent with the SEM results.

XPS survey was conducted to determine the chemical compositions of the three N-CDs (Fig. 4 ). The pronounced peaks at around 284.5 eV, 400 eV, and 531.2 eV correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The deconvolution of C1s surveys suggest three distinguish peaks including CC/C–C, C–O/C–N and CO/CN [31] for all the three N-CDs. Their corresponding N1s high-resolution spectra can also be fitted into three peaks, which can be attributed to pyridinic-like N (398.9 eV for N-cCDs, 398.4 eV for N-lCDs, 398.8 eV for N-xCDs), pyrrolic-like N/amino N (400 eV for N-cCDs, 399.7 eV for N-lCDs, 399.7 eV for N-xCDs), and “graphitic” N (401.2 eV for N-cCDs, 400.9 eV for N-lCDs, 400.5 eV for N-xCDs) [28]. Two strong peaks can be fitted for O1s spectra of the three N-CDs, being assigned to the CO and C–O, proving abundance oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the three N-CDs. Surprisingly, the characteristic peak of CO cannot be detected in the FT-IR spectra, which is possibly due to the hyperconjugation between the functional groups [32] and graphitic-likes carbon core. Concluding the TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS results, it is reasonable to believe that the obtained products contain N-doped sp2, sp3 hybrid carbon core. Moreover, the carbon core is surrounded by oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, and some of them are conjugated with the sp2 hybrid carbon core.

The optical properties of the three CDs and N-CDs were characterized by fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 , it is interesting to observe two excitation peaks and one emission peak in all CDs and N-CDs. For the three CDs and N-CDs, excited by the left side of both peak 1 and peak 2 (200–230 nm for peak 1, 280–320 nm for peak 2), their FL intensity increased with the excitation wavelength, and show the excited-independent property. However, as excitation wavelength ranging from 230 nm–270 nm and 320 nm–400 nm (right-hand side of excitation peak 1 and peak 2, respectively), their fluorescence exhibit negatively correlated with excitation wavelength, meanwhile reveal the excited-dependent property. As expected, the optimal excitation wavelength was located at the crest of the two excitation bands.

The stokes shift (Δν) of CDs can be calculated by the equation below [33]:
Δν = νex – νem
Where, νex and νem are the optimal excitation and emission (cm−1), respectively. A large stokes shift around 19669 cm−1 for the three CDs and N-CDs were obtained, and it was larger than those previously reported CDs (summarize in Table 2 ). Large stokes shift, which eliminates the distractions between the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum, ensures a higher signal-to-noise ratio and prevents the self-quenching [34]. Excited at the short wavelength can provide high energy, and enable exciting electrons to be transited to higher vibration level, thus reducing emission energy, increasing stokes shifts and decreasing FL intensity [34]. For this reason, the emission intensity of the three CDs obtained by excitation peaks 1 was weaker than that obtained by peak 2. However, after N-doping, the fluorescence obtained by excitation peak 1 showed the same or even higher emission intensity than that of excitation peak 2, which will be discussed later.
The novel double excitation-one emission phenomenon and the dramatic enhancement of fluorescence after N-doping inspired us to explore their fluorescence mechanism (Fig. 6 ).

The novel double excitation - one emission phenomenon for the three CDs and N-CDs was discussed firstly. The excited band at 230 nm may be result from the π-π* transition of sp2 hybrid carbon core, that at 320 nm may be related to the surface functional groups. By exciting at both 230 nm and 320 nm, the maximum emission centers are almost unchanged for the three CDs and N-CDs, indicating that N-doping unchanged the fluorescence centers of CDs. We speculate that the double excitation-one emission phenomenon may be the result of the hyperconjugation between the surface CO, CN functional groups and the sp2 hybrid carbon core. This speculation can be supported by the FT-IR and XPS study.

Then the fluorescence substantial enhancement of the three CDs by N-doping was discussed. As illustrates in Fig. 7 a, with the introduction of NH4HCO3 the fluorescence of cCDs, lCDs, and xCDs were enhanced 3.64 times, 2.66 times, and 3.9 times, respectively. Guo et al. [40] claimed that the formation of CO2 and NH3 produced by NH4HCO3 decomposition would increase the pressure of the autoclave, which was conducive to the decomposition of carbon source, and the fluorescence of CDs was thus enhanced significantly. In this study, the effect of different nitrogen sources, including NH4HCO3, NH4OH, CON2H4, and C2H4N4 on the FL intensity of N-CDs have been compared by fixing the nitrogen content (Fig. 7 b). In this condition, NH4HCO3 can produce the most gas to improve the reaction pressure. If the reaction pressure plays a vital role, the fluorescence of NH4HCO3 associated CDs will provide the most fluorescence. Experiment result showed that the best nitrogen source for cCDs, lCDs, and xCDs was C2H4N4, CON2H4, and CON2H4, respectively. To our surprise, NH4HCO3 was not the best nitrogen source for all the three CDs. Therefore, the FL intensity significant enhancement of the three CDs by NH4HCO3 in this work was not caused by increasing of reaction pressure. The introduction of nitrogen was the best reason to explain the significant increase in CDs fluorescence, which have been discussed by previous report [41].

Interestingly, the FL intensities of the three N-CDs excited by 230 nm and 320 nm were significantly increased compared with the three CDs. These results indicate that nitrogen acted on both carbon core and functional groups [42,43]. In carbon core, N presents as pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N, and “graphitic” N [44]. The “graphitic” N can replace C in the sp2 carbon core, pyridinic-like N and pyrrolic-like N produce defects to the carbon core, and increase the isolated sp2 structure, thus enhance the FL intensity of the three N-CDs [24]. N may exist as –CN and amides in the surrounding functional groups. –CN contributed the conjugation system to the sp2 carbon core, and amides can donate electrons to the –CO via its lone pair electrons to increase the conjugation of –CO to sp2 carbon core. Besides, the fluorescence excited at 230 nm is more stable than that excited at 320 nm for the three N-CDs. This can be attributed to the large stokes shift, which can reduce or even avoid self-quenching and re-emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH and high salt concentration on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability of N-CDs in different pH and high salt environment plays a key role in its practical applications. To understand their environmental stability, the FL intensity of the three N-CDs in different pH conditions were investigated. Some CDs show pH-dependent property, which may be possibly due to the protonation-deprotonation of the surface functional groups [45]. However, in our study, the emission intensity revealed the neglected change in pH 1–12 for N-cCDs, 3–11 for N-lCDs, 4–11 for N-xCDs, suggesting the incredible pH stability of CDs (Fig. 8 a, their corresponding fluorescence spectra display in Fig. S3). The good pH stability may be attributed to the conjugate effect between the functional groups and graphitic-likes carbon core, which may inhibit the protonation-deprotonation process. Furthermore, the three N-CDs were suffered from the treatment of NaCl ranging from 0.0 M to 1.0 M. As shown in Fig. 8b, the concentration of NaCl showed negligible effect on the FL intensity of the three N-CDs, indicating their potential application in the high salt environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential applications of N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs

ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 9 shows the temperature-dependent FL intensity of the three as-prepared CDs. The FL quenching intensity of N-cCDs and N-xCDs showed good linear relationships at 9 oC–80 oC with the regression equations of ΔF = 2.82T–24.44 (r = 0.9982), ΔF = 3.39 T–13.18 (r = 0.998), respectively. Where ΔF = F
9
°C - F
x
°C, T is the temperature, °C. Above result suggests the promising application in temperature sensing for N-cCDs and N-xCDs. For N-lCDs, its fluorescence showed closely unchanged in the temperature ranges of 9 °C–30 °C and 35 °C–80 °C, while raising sharply at 30–35 °C (Fig. 9 inset). As temperature went up from 9 °C to 80 °C, the emission spectra of the three N-CDs did not shift within the investigated temperature window.

Fig. 10 a displays the photograph of the three N-CDs under 365 nm UV light. The bright-white fluorescence can be observed for N-cCDs and N-xCDs, while the N-lCDs shows weak fluorescence due to its low QY. As exhibits in Fig. 10b, the three N-CDs solutions were utilized to write directly on the paper, they show brightly colour under 365 nm UV light. Furthermore, unlike traditional organic fluorescence ink, which generally suffer from the drawback of the toxicity, the N-CDs based ink is eco-friendly, low-toxicity, easy-washed, and with excellent biocompatibility. Fig. 10c reveals the application of the three N-CDs on eiderdown, cotton, and silk identification. Eiderdown, silk and cotton show no fluorescence in N-lCDs under 254 nm UV light due to its weak fluorescence, thus N-lCDs cannot be used to identify these fabric. In the presence of N-cCDs and N-xCDs, both silk and cotton show fluorescence under 254 nm UV light, while eiderdown show no fluorescence, thus eiderdown can be identified. Experiment results further show that under 365 nm UV light, the original silk reveal bright fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, while original cotton and eiderdown do not reveal fluorescence, thus silk can be identified.

With the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence of all the three N-CDs were decreased gradually (Fig.11a~c). The most critical parameter to judge the fluorescence quenching mechanism is the average fluorescence lifetime (τavg). Fig. S4 and Table S1 show that the τavg of N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs is 6.62, 5.00, and 6.42 ns, respectively. With the introduction of Hg2+, their corresponding lifetime is 6.65, 0.88, and 6.50 ns, respectively. The nearly constant τavg of N-cCDs and N-xCDs imply a static fluorescence quenching process [15,39,41]. The fluorescence quenching provoked of N-cCDs and N-xCDs by Hg2+ was probably due to the formation of nonfluorescence complex between Hg2+ and the surface functional groups of N-cCDs and N-xCDs. However, the remarkably reduced lifetime of N-lCDs in the presence of Hg2+ indicates the specific dynamic quenching [15,46]. The fluorescence quenching of N-lCDs by Hg2+ was assigned to a fast electron transfer process. Furthermore, the similar lifetime of N-cCDs and N-xCDs, and their similar behavior to Hg2+ imply that they underwent the similar luminescence process [8]. The difference lifetime of N-lCDs and its different behavior to Hg2+ imply its different luminescence process from N-cCDs and N-xCDs.

As displayed in Fig. 11 d, the fluorescence quenching ratios F
0
/F, in which F
0 and F are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of Hg2+, are in good linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+, with linear regression equations of F
0
/F = 1.06 + 0.0038 C, F
0
/F = 0.90 + 0.0024 C, and F
0
/F = 0.83 + 0.0050 C, and correlation coefficients (r) of 0.9950, 0.9973 and 0.9985 for N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs, respectively. Based on these, three fluorescence sensors for Hg2+ detecting were constructed. N-xCDs exhibits the best performance in detecting Hg2+ among the three N-CDs, which is possibly attributed to its excellent fluorescence and abundant functional groups on the surface. The detection parameters, including linear range and detection limit (LOD) for the three Hg2+ sensors and other Hg2+ sensors reported before [15,24,25,37,42,47] are summarized in Table S2. According to Table S2, the three Hg2+ sensors constructed in this work are comparable to or even better than that reported before.

To verify the selectivity of the three Hg2+ sensors, interference experiments in the presence of different interfering ions (including Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, As3+, and Pb2+) were performed under the same experiment conditions. The influence of interference ions was evaluated by calculating F0/F value, where F0 and F are the FL intensities of absence and presence of interference ions. It can be seen from Fig. 11e that by fixing their concentration at 1 μM, the F0/F values of Hg2+ for N-cCDs, N-lCDs, and N-xCDs are 4.86, 3.41, 6.01, respectively, and that for interfering ions are in the ranges of 1.02–1.57, 1.05–1.82, 1.02–1.78, respectively. The F0/F value of blank samples is 1.00. Results show that the three N-CDs are the most sensitive to Hg2+ compared to other metal ions.

In the present work, comparison of N-CDs produced from three main components of the plants, including cellulose, xylose, and lignin was proceeding. Results showed that fluorescence properties of N-cCDs and N-xCDs were similar, while that of N-lCDs was difference. Surprisingly, the novel double excitation-one emission phenomenon was observed, which might be contributed to the conjugated effect between functional groups and carbon core. Results showed great improvement of the fluorescence obtained by both two excitation peaks after N-doping. We speculated that N in the forms of pyridinic-like N and pyrrolic-like N would increase the isolated sp2 structure of carbon core, enabling the effective fluorescence enhancement excited at 230 nm. For the surrounding function groups, N improved the conjugation between functional groups and carbon core, and then increased the fluorescence of N-CDs excited at 320 nm. The three N-CDs show pronounced stability in pH and salt solution. Outstanding fluorescence properties of N-cCDs and N-xCDs permitted them potentially application in fluorescence ink and fabrics identification. Moreover, the N-CDs show their potentiality in temperature and Hg2+ sensing.
Xiufen Liao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data Curation, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review & Editing, Project administration.
Congjin Chen: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
Ruxia Zhou: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing.
Quanlong huang: Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing.
Qinglong Liang: Software, Writing-Original Draft, Visualization.
Zuqiang Huang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision.
Yanjuan Zhang: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.
Huayu Hu: Resources, Supervision.
Yanglin Liang: Validation, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660183), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2015GXNSFAA139023), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (XJPZ160713).
